summary: Rent the Runway faces a significant business challenge: women are hesitant to rent dresses because of fit uncertainty, especially with items arriving close to their events. While engineers typically propose algorithmic solutions like 3D modeling and virtual fit assistants, these technologies have failed to gain traction because they strip the emotional element from the shopping experience. The company discovered through data that photos of real women wearing dresses are far more effective than technical solutions. This insight led to the launch of 'Our Runway,' a feature letting women shop by viewing other real customers in the dresses, triggering emotional connection rather than algorithmic confidence.
key_points: Engineers instinctively jump to algorithmic solutions (3D modeling, computer vision) for fit problems, but these have consistently failed to gain user adoption
Virtual fit technology falls into the uncanny valley - it undermines rather than builds confidence because it doesn't look enough like real people
The fit problem is fundamentally emotional, not algorithmic - women need to feel good about how something will look, not just know measurements
Photos of real women wearing dresses are more effective because the human brain excels at visual tasks and emotional identification
Data-driven insights combined with emotional reasoning produced a better solution than pure technical approaches
money_quotes: What engineers often forget when faced with a problem is the emotional element of that problem.
Fit seems like an algorithmic problem, but for many women, there is a huge emotional component to trying things on.
Turning fit into a cheesy 3D model strips all emotion from the experience, and puts it into the uncanny valley of not-quite-realness.
Don't forget that the human brain is still much more powerful than computers at visual tasks.
It is much easier for us to imagine ourselves in an item of clothing when we see it on many other women.
Learning to use emotional reasoning was a revelation to me, and it might be the most valuable engineering insight I've picked up in the last year.
key_insight: The best engineering solutions often require understanding the emotional dimensions of a problem rather than jumping to purely technical approaches.
